title: Somatic Therapy for Survivors of Childhood Emotional Neglect by Functional Medicine Culture
excerpt: Understand how somatic therapy heals childhood emotional neglect by addressing body-based trauma and nervous system regulation.
body:
Understanding Childhood Emotional Neglect (CEN)

Childhood Emotional Neglect (CEN) is a subtle, often overlooked form of trauma where a child's emotional needs are consistently unmet or ignored. Unlike overt abuse, CEN doesn’t leave visible scars but deeply impacts self-awareness, emotional regulation, and attachment. Dr. Jonice Webb, a pioneer in this field, describes CEN as the absence of emotional support, which causes survivors to feel invisible or unworthy of care.1 Many who grew up with CEN describe feeling disconnected from their own emotions, a core challenge that keeps them stuck despite traditional talk therapies.

Why Traditional Therapy Often Falls Short

Traditional psychotherapy, which is largely cognitive and verbal, can fail survivors of CEN because the trauma is stored in the body and nervous system rather than in explicit memories. As trauma researcher Bessel van der Kolk points out, "Trauma is stored in nonverbal areas of the brain and nervous system; it is encoded in bodily sensations and affects regulation."2 Without addressing the body's implicit memory, talk therapy alone may not resolve the internal disconnection or chronic dysregulation that survivors experience.

What Is Somatic Therapy?

Somatic therapy is a body-centered approach that helps survivors access, feel, and regulate bodily sensations linked to trauma. It is grounded in the work of Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, and Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy. These modalities focus on the nervous system and the body’s natural capacity to heal when given appropriate resources and safety.3,4 Through mindful awareness of the body, survivors can begin to reclaim the parts of themselves that were neglected or disowned during childhood.

The Nervous System and Emotional Neglect

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory is foundational to somatic therapy's understanding of trauma. It explains how the autonomic nervous system responds to threat by shifting between states of social engagement, fight/flight, or shutdown.5 In survivors of CEN, the nervous system often defaults to states of hypo-arousal or dissociation—symptoms of emotional disconnection. This leads to challenges in self-soothing and emotional regulation, perpetuating feelings of emptiness and numbness.

Deb Dana, a leading clinician in polyvagal-informed therapy, emphasizes the importance of tracking bodily sensations to shift from defensive states toward safety and connection.6

How Somatic Therapy Addresses CEN

Somatic therapy offers tools to identify and regulate the nervous system’s response to emotional neglect. Key elements include:

  • Body Awareness and Tracking: Learning to notice subtle sensations, like tightness or warmth, that signal emotional states.
  • Resourcing and Safe Anchors: Developing internal and external resources that evoke a sense of safety, such as grounding techniques or visualization.
  • Pendulation: Alternating between states of activation and calm to help the nervous system process and release stuck energy.
  • Touch and Movement: Gentle somatic interventions such as breathwork, movement, or therapeutic touch can activate the parasympathetic nervous system and foster regulation.

By working through the body, survivors gradually reconnect with their emotions and build capacity for self-compassion and connection.

Integrating Somatic Therapy with Other Modalities

Somatic therapy often complements other trauma-informed approaches such as Internal Family Systems (IFS) by Richard Schwartz and Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) by Francine Shapiro. While IFS helps survivors identify and heal fragmented parts of the self, EMDR facilitates processing of traumatic memories, somatic therapy grounds these processes in the body.7,8,9 Functional Medicine Culture advocates for an integrative approach that combines nervous system regulation, somatic awareness, and psychotherapy to address the full spectrum of CEN's impact.

Self-Directed Somatic Practices for Survivors

For those on a self-healing journey, incorporating daily somatic practices can accelerate recovery:

  • Body Scanning: Spend 5-10 minutes daily noticing sensations without judgment.
  • Breath Awareness: Practice slow, diaphragmatic breathing to engage the vagus nerve.
  • Grounding Exercises: Engage the senses by noticing textures, sounds, or smells to anchor in the present.
  • Movement: Gentle yoga or stretching can release tension and improve body-mind connection.

Consistency is key. These practices gradually rewire the nervous system toward safety and emotional attunement.

Why Healing CEN Is Possible—and Necessary

“Healing from childhood emotional neglect is not about finding blame but reclaiming the parts of ourselves lost to invisibility.” — Functional Medicine Culture

With somatic therapy, survivors of CEN can finally access the emotional life that was denied to them. Dr. Gabor Maté emphasizes that trauma healing requires compassionate presence with the body’s sensations and experiences.10 Without this body-based work, survivors remain trapped in patterns of disconnection, self-neglect, and emotional numbness. Somatic therapy offers a path to reclaim wholeness, presence, and authentic connection.

References

  • 1. Webb, J. (2013). Running on Empty: Overcome Your Childhood Emotional Neglect.
  • 2. van der Kolk, B. (2014). The Body Keeps the Score.
  • 3. Levine, P. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice: How the Body Releases Trauma.
  • 4. Ogden, P., Minton, K., & Pain, C. (2006). Trauma and the Body: A Sensorimotor Approach to Psychotherapy.
  • 5. Porges, S. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory: Neurophysiological Foundations of Emotions, Attachment, Communication.
  • 6. Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy.
  • 7. Schwartz, R. (1995). Internal Family Systems Therapy.
  • 8. Shapiro, F. (2017). Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) Therapy.
  • 9. Siegel, D. (2012). The Developing Mind.
  • 10. Maté, G. (2018). In the Realm of Hungry Ghosts.

faqs.a: Childhood emotional neglect occurs when a child's emotional needs are consistently ignored or unmet, leading to difficulties in emotional awareness and regulation.

faqs.q: How does somatic therapy help with emotional neglect?
faqs.a: Somatic therapy works by helping survivors reconnect with bodily sensations, regulate their nervous system, and process trauma stored in the body rather than relying solely on talking.

faqs.q: Can I practice somatic therapy techniques on my own?
faqs.a: Yes, basic somatic practices like body scanning, breath awareness, and grounding can be done independently, but working with a trained therapist is recommended for deeper trauma work.

faqs.q: How is somatic therapy different from traditional talk therapy?
faqs.a: Somatic therapy focuses on the body and nervous system to address trauma, whereas traditional talk therapy centers mostly on cognitive and verbal processing.

affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book that explains how trauma is stored in the body and offers insight into somatic approaches for healing.
